title: Reading and Use of English – Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about the history of the Library of Alexandria. Choose from paragraphs A–G which fits each gap. There is one extra paragraph.

content.articleTitle: The Lost Treasure of Knowledge: The Great Library
content.text: In the ancient world, the Library of Alexandria was the ultimate symbol of intellectual ambition. Founded by the Ptolemaic dynasty in Egypt during the 3rd century BC, it was intended to be a universal storehouse for all human knowledge. At its peak, it is estimated to have held hundreds of thousands of papyrus scrolls, covering everything from Greek poetry to Babylonian mathematics. ||(41)|| [41]This aggressive acquisition strategy was fueled by the city’s position as a Mediterranean hub[/41]. The Ptolemies were so obsessed with expanding the collection that they famously ordered all ships docking in the harbor to be searched for books. Any scrolls found were confiscated, copied by professional scribes, and then—more often than not—the copies were returned while the originals were kept for the library. ||(42)|| [42]This vast archive made Alexandria the undisputed center of the Hellenistic world's scientific and literary life[/42]. Great minds like Eratosthenes, who calculated the Earth’s circumference, and Aristarchus, who first proposed a heliocentric universe, walked its halls. They were supported by royal stipends, allowing them to focus entirely on their research without the distraction of earning a living. ||(43)|| [43]However, the library’s decline was not a single, dramatic event, despite the popular image of a great fire started by Julius Caesar[/43]. While a fire did break out during Caesar’s occupation of the city in 48 BC, it likely only destroyed a portion of the collection stored near the docks. The true end was a slow process of neglect, dwindling budgets, and religious volatility. ||(44)|| [44]As the Roman Empire transitioned toward Christianity, the classical learning housed in Alexandria began to be viewed with suspicion[/44]. In 391 AD, Emperor Theodosius ordered the destruction of pagan temples, and it is believed that much of what remained of the library’s daughter collection in the Serapeum was lost during the resulting riots. ||(45)|| [45]This gradual disappearance of the world’s greatest archive had a profound impact on history[/45]. Countless works by Sophocles, Euripides, and Aristarchus are known to us only by their titles, mentioned in the few fragments that survived elsewhere. The loss of these texts effectively created a 'dark age' in our understanding of ancient science and philosophy. ||(46)|| [46]Today, the Bibliotheca Alexandrina stands near the site of the original, a modern tribute to the ancient dream of universal knowledge[/46]. While we can never recover the lost scrolls, the spirit of the library lives on in the digital age, where the quest to organize the world’s information continues on a scale the Ptolemies could never have imagined.

paragraphs.B: Indeed, [41] the scale of the collection [/41] was maintained through a variety of unorthodox methods. Agents were sent to book fairs in Athens and Rhodes with unlimited budgets to buy up entire libraries, ensuring that Alexandria remained the premier destination for any serious scholar of the era.
paragraphs.A: This [42] ruthless pursuit of the written word [/42] ensured that the library’s shelves were constantly replenished. It wasn't just about quantity, though; the librarians also developed the first sophisticated systems of categorization and philology, creating the prototypes for the modern bibliography.
paragraphs.G: Beyond [43] these individual academic achievements [/43], the library served a broader political purpose. It was a tool of 'soft power,' demonstrating that the Greek rulers of Egypt were the rightful heirs to the intellectual legacy of Alexander the Great and the true protectors of civilization.
paragraphs.C: This [44] prolonged period of instability [/44] meant that the scrolls were no longer being maintained or copied. Papyrus is a fragile material that rots easily in humid conditions; without constant care and the patronage of the state, the library’s contents literally began to crumble into dust.
paragraphs.E: The [45] final blow to the institution [/45] is often attributed to the Arab conquest in the 7th century, though many historians now argue that the library had effectively ceased to exist long before then. The lack of contemporary accounts suggests that by the time the city changed hands, the great halls were already empty.
paragraphs.D: This [46] cultural vacuum [/46] was felt for over a millennium. The loss of technical manuals on engineering and medicine meant that much of the practical knowledge of the Greeks and Romans had to be painfully rediscovered by later generations during the Renaissance.
paragraphs.F: Many [47] modern archaeologists [/47] are still searching for the physical remains of the library building. Because the ancient coastline of Alexandria has subsided into the sea, it is possible that the foundations of the 'Great Hall' now lie underwater, waiting to be explored by scuba divers.

questions.explanation: B refers to the 'scale of the collection' and the methods used to build it, linking directly to the 'aggressive acquisition strategy' mentioned after the gap.
A links the 'ruthless pursuit' (searching ships and keeping originals) to the development of the library's internal systems of 'categorization'.
G refers back to the 'individual academic achievements' of Eratosthenes and Aristarchus and explains the broader 'political purpose' of the library.
C explains how the 'prolonged period of instability' and lack of 'patronage' led to the physical decay of the fragile papyrus scrolls.
E discusses the 'final blow' and the lack of accounts, connecting the religious riots described before the gap to the eventual total disappearance of the library.
D refers to the 'cultural vacuum' created by the loss of the texts, explaining the 'dark age' and the need for rediscovery mentioned in the text.
